coredata.dc:title: Molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor based on Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs for detecting the gastric cancer marker p-Hydroxybenzoic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Jie, Rui
Zhu, Xiaochen
Qi, Haiyan
Jing, Tao
Li, Jun
Zhao, Yunpeng
Yu, Jinzhi
Qi, Chenyu
coredata.dc:description: With the rapid development of metabolomics, p-Hydroxybenzoic acid in urine has been identified as a crucial biomarker for the early detection and screening of gastric cancer. In this study, we designed and synthesized a novel magnetic hollow carbon dot molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor (Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs) to achieve high sensitivity and selectivity detection of this biomarker. The incorporation of Fe3O4 nanoparticles into the sensor significantly improved its recognition capability and analytical performance. The fluorescence sensor exhibited a strong linear response within the concentration range of 0–100 μmol/L, with an impressive detection limit of 0.37 μmol/L. The sensor demonstrated excellent specificity, showing minimal interference from common cations, anions, amino acids, and structural analogs, underscoring its high selectivity for p-Hydroxybenzoic acid. In practical applications, spike recovery experiments conducted on urine samples from both gastric cancer patients and healthy individuals revealed recovery rates ranging from 95.00 % to 106.67 % and relative standard deviations between 0.76 % and 2.80 %. These results confirmed the sensor’s outstanding accuracy, reproducibility, and stability. Overall, the Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs sensor provides a robust, non-invasive instrument for detecting gastric cancer biomarkers in urine, offering significant potential for early diagnosis and monitoring of gastric cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecular Imprinting Polymers
Carbon dots
p-Hydroxybenzoic acid
Gastric cancer marker

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and stability of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs
Detection of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid using Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs and mechanism analysis
Evaluation of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs in Real urine samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-b) TEM (c) SEM image of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs. (d) SEM image of Fe3O4/CDs@NIPs. (e-h) SEM images and elemental mapping of C, N, O, Si, and Zn for Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs.

Characterization of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs. (a)XRD. (b) FT-IR spectra comparing Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs and Fe3O4/CDs@NIPs. (c) XPS. (d-e) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and corresponding pore size distributions. (f) TGA curves. (g) Hysteresis regression lines for Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs.

Optical properties and stability of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs. (a) Fluorescence spectrum. (b) Fluorescence spectrum at excitation wavelengths ranging from 310 nm to 400 nm. (c) Normalized fluorescence spectrum. (d) UV–Vis absorption spectrum. (e) pH sensitivity. (f) Salt tolerance. (g) UV Light stability.

Optimization and fluorescence response of Fe3O4/CDs @MIPs for p-Hydroxybenzoic acid detection. (a) Selection of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs concentration. (b) Testing environment for Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs evaluation. (c) Optimal response time. (d) Emission spectra of different concentrations of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid (inset: Fe3O4/CDs@NIPs). (e) Standard curves for the detection of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid for Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs and Fe3O4/CDs@NIPs. (f) UV absorption spectra. (g) Fluorescent decay curves.

Selectivity and Anti-Interference Tests of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs. (a-b) Selectivity. (c-d) Anti-interference experiments where Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs were exposed to a mixture of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid and various interfering substances.
Schematic Illustration of the Preparation Process for Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs.
Recovery Rates of p-Hydroxybenzoic Acid in Urine Samples from Gastric Cancer Patients and Healthy Individuals.

head.ce:title: Molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor based on Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs for detecting the gastric cancer marker p-Hydroxybenzoic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, No. 42, Wenhua Street, Qiqihar 161006, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the rapid development of metabolomics, p-Hydroxybenzoic acid in urine has been identified as a crucial biomarker for the early detection and screening of gastric cancer. In this study, we designed and synthesized a novel magnetic hollow carbon dot molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor (Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs) to achieve high sensitivity and selectivity detection of this biomarker. The incorporation of Fe3O4 nanoparticles into the sensor significantly improved its recognition capability and analytical performance. The fluorescence sensor exhibited a strong linear response within the concentration range of 0–100 μmol/L, with an impressive detection limit of 0.37 μmol/L. The sensor demonstrated excellent specificity, showing minimal interference from common cations, anions, amino acids, and structural analogs, underscoring its high selectivity for p-Hydroxybenzoic acid. In practical applications, spike recovery experiments conducted on urine samples from both gastric cancer patients and healthy individuals revealed recovery rates ranging from 95.00 % to 106.67 % and relative standard deviations between 0.76 % and 2.80 %. These results confirmed the sensor’s outstanding accuracy, reproducibility, and stability. Overall, the Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs sensor provides a robust, non-invasive instrument for detecting gastric cancer biomarkers in urine, offering significant potential for early diagnosis and monitoring of gastric cancer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Gastric cancer ranks among the most prevalent and lethal malignancies of the digestive system, with a particularly high incidence in China [1,2]. In 2002, approximately 42 % of all global gastric cancer cases were reported in China, underscoring the urgency for improved early detection strategies in the region [3]. Despite advancements in screening methodologies, conventional techniques such as endoscopy and imaging often suffer from limitations like low sensitivity, poor specificity, and limited accessibility, thereby reducing their overall effectiveness in early diagnosis [4,5].

The emergence of metabolomics offering new opportunities for non-invasive diagnostic approaches. People have identified several urinary biomarkers with significant potential for early gastric cancer detection [6]. Among these, p-Hydroxybenzoic acid [7,8] has garnered attention due to its promising diagnostic accuracy [9,10]. Urinary biomarkers present distinct advantages: they enable straightforward, minimally invasive sample collection, and reduce patient discomfort, making them particularly suitable for large-scale clinical applications [11].

Currently, multiple analytical methods, such as gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS), ion chromatography, and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), are employed for the quantification of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid [12]. While these techniques offer high precision and reliability, they often involve complex sample preparation, lengthy processing times, and high operational costs. Hence, there is a growing demand for more efficient and selective detection systems.

In recent years, molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensors have turned into promising alternatives due to their remarkable stability, high specificity, and sensitive fluorescence detection capabilities [13–18]. These sensors offer significant improvements over conventional techniques in terms of detection efficiency, selectivity, and cost-effectiveness. Among fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered considerable attention for their exceptional properties, including excellent water solubility, fluorescence stability, and environmental compatibility [19–22]. Various CDs-based molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensors have been successfully developed for biomarker detection in biological fluids such as plasma and urine [23,24]. However, these existing CDs-based sensors face challenges such as suboptimal selectivity, relatively high detection limits, and extended detection times. To address these limitations, the integration of functional materials into molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) is being explored to enhance detection performance [25,26]. Magnetic nanomaterials, particularly Fe3O4, offer unique advantages for sensor development. They possess distinctive surface physicochemical properties, are relatively easy to synthesize, and exhibit superparamagnetic behavior, facilitating rapid and efficient separation processes [27]. Incorporating Fe3O4 into MIPs not only streamlines sample preparation and purification but also enables selective target enrichment, thereby enhancing analytical efficiency and overall detection performance [28–30].

In this study, we developed a novel magnetic hollow carbon dots molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor (Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs) using a sol–gel synthesis approach (Scheme 1 ). This sensor incorporates N, S-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) as the fluorophore for enhanced fluorescence sensitivity and stability, Fe3O4 nanoparticles as the magnetic unit for rapid separation and target enrichment, and zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) as a structural support material to improve robustness. The functional monomer 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and the cross-linking agent tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) facilitate the formation of a stable polymer matrix, with ammonia acting as the initiator. Specific molecular recognition sites were created within the imprinted polymer using p-Hydroxybenzoic acid as the template molecule. Comprehensive characterization confirmed the successful synthesis and integration of the sensor components. The resulting sensor demonstrated high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, and a low detection limit for p-Hydroxybenzoic acid, with its detection mechanism relying on specific molecular recognition. Practical applicability was then validated through successful detection of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid in human urine samples. These findings indicate the sensor's potential as a non-invasive, reliable diagnostic tool for early gastric cancer detection, paving the way for improved clinical screening and management strategies.
The relevant information about experimental materials and instruments can be found in the Supplementary data.

First, 2.0 g of sodium acetate and 1.5 g of FeCl3·6H2O were dissolved in 50 mL of ethylene glycol. The solution was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) liner, which was then placed into an autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 7 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting product centrifuged and dried to obtain the purified Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

0.03 g of N, S-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs), 0.04 g of ZIF-8, and 0.04 g of Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed in 40 mL of absolute ethanol. Next, 400 μL of 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and 400 μL of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were added sequentially, with continuous stirring. After 30 min, 1 mL of ammonia was introduced to initiate polymerization, and the reaction was allowed to proceed under mechanical stirring for 12 h. The final product was collected by centrifugation, washed three times with ultrapure water and absolute ethanol, and dried at 60 °C to obtain Fe3O4/CDs composite material.

For the synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), 0.1381 g of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid was dissolved in 40 mL of absolute ethanol and sonicated for 10 min to achieve complete dispersion. Subsequently, 708 μL of APTES was added, and the mixture was stirred mechanically for 30 min. Then, 150 mg of the previously synthesized Fe3O4/CDs composite was introduced into the solution and stirred for an additional 10 min. Following this, 1776 μL of TEOS and 1 mL of ammonia were added to initiate polymerization, and the reaction mixture was stirred continuously for 12 h. Upon completion, the product was collected by centrifugation, washed three times with absolute ethanol, and dried at 60 °C. To remove the template molecule, the resulting material was eluted with a methanol: acetic acid (9:1 v/v) solution by centrifugation until the p-Hydroxybenzoic acid content in the supernatant was negligible. The product was subsequently washed with anhydrous methanol until a neutral pH was achieved and then dried at 60 °C to yield Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs. For control experiments, a non-imprinted polymer (NIPs) version (Fe3O4/CDs@NIPs) was synthesized using the same procedure but without the addition of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid as the template molecule. This NIP served as a reference to evaluate the selectivity and specificity of the MIPs-based sensor.

In our previous experiments, we identified the optimal dosages of ZIF-8 and N,S-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) for the sensor development. However, the concentration of Fe3O4 nanoparticles plays a crucial role in determining the internal spatial structure of the molecularly imprinted polymer (MIPs) and, consequently, the performance of the fluorescence sensor. To determine the optimal concentration of Fe3O4, we conducted a single-factor optimization study.

Firstly, the prepared Fe3O4, CDs and ZIF-8 were coated with TEOS. Fe3O4 and CDs were evenly distributed on the outer surface of ZIF-8, and the pore adjustability and high specific surface area of ZIF-8 were used to improve the detection performance of the sensor. Then, the CDs were silanized by TEOS using the Stöber method. Among them, SiO2 can protect the fluorescence performance of CDs, improve biocompatibility, dispersion and surface functionalization, and it also promote the occurrence of the next polymerization reaction. Finally, Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs was prepared by sol–gel method.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1
a-b) revealed spherical aggregates with consistent size and shape, typical of hybrid materials. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Fig. 1 c-d) further demonstrated that the material retained a uniform surface morphology. A comparison with ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs ( Fig. S3 ) highlighted a distinct transition from a lighter to a darker core, indicating the successful incorporation of Fe3O4 nanoparticles onto the ZIF-8 surface. The presence of a transparent SiO2 shell around the composite confirmed the formation of the silica layer, which acts as an additional protective coating. EDS (Fig. 1 e-h) confirmed the successful assembly of the material, showing the uniform distribution of elements such as C, N, O, Si, Zn, and Fe. The homogeneous distribution of these elements is critical for ensuring the reliability and consistency of the fluorescence sensor, indicating proper integration of all components.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Fig. 2
a) displayed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 23°, corresponding to the C (002) plane, which is indicative of the successful deposition of the amorphous silica (SiO2) layer [31]. Additionally, four distinct diffraction peaks at 220, 311, 511, and 440 within the 2θ range of 25–70° were observed, which are characteristic of the Fe3O4 crystal structure, confirming the presence of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [32]. In FT-IR (Fig. 2 b) further supported the formation of the SiO2 layer and the successful incorporation of functional groups. Specifically, characteristic peaks at 3425 cm−1, 1157 cm−1, and 791 cm−1 (–OH, C-O, and Si-C), respectively [33,34]. Peaks at 2933 cm−1 and 1069 cm−1 corresponded to C-H and Si-O-Si stretching vibrations, while the peak at 1572 cm−1 indicated N-H bending vibrations. The absence of N-H peaks at 1572 cm−1 and 1414 cm−1 in the non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) suggested that reactions occurred with acids during the elution process, further supporting the formation of the desired material. In XPS (Fig. 2 c and
S4 ) was employed to analyze the surface composition of the composite. The spectra confirmed the presence of Fe3O4 and silica components, and provided further insights into the oxidation states of the various elements, validating the successful integration of Fe3O4 and silica into the composite structure [34].

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm and pore size distribution are shown in Fig. 2 d-e. The specific surface area of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs was 7.6339 m2/g, the pore volume was 0.0316 cm2/g, and the average pore size was 16.5343 nm, indicating that the material had a mesoporous structure (2–50 nm). A higher specific surface area indicates that the polymer has a large adsorption capacity, indicating that the introduction of Fe3O4 nanoparticles further improves the adsorption performance of carbon dot molecularly imprinted polymers.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Fig. 2 f) showed a total weight loss of 32.22 % over the temperature range of 30–800 °C, with distinct stages corresponding to water volatilization, active group pyrolysis, and ZIF-8 decomposition. Specifically, the weight loss rate of the composite material was 4.47 % in the first stage (30–118 °C), which may be attributed to the volatilization of free water and residual solvents. The second stage (118–200 °C) showed a weight loss of 8.32 %, likely due to the pyrolysis of active groups, while the third stage (200–800 °C) exhibited a weight loss rate of 19.43 %, indicating the decomposition of ZIF-8. Overall, the thermal stability observed suggests that the Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs sensor can withstand high temperatures, making it suitable for use in diverse environmental conditions. Magnetic properties were evaluated through hysteresis loop measurements (Fig. 2 g). The saturation magnetization values of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs were found to be 79.82 emu/g and 6.84 emu/g, respectively. The symmetrical hysteresis curves for both Fe3O4 nanoparticles and the composite material suggest that both are superparamagnetic, with no significant hysteresis. The reduced magnetic strength of the fluorescence sensor could be due to the formation of the silica layer around the Fe3O4 nanoparticles, which may have a diminishing effect on the material’s overall magnetization. In summary, these comprehensive characterization results confirm the successful synthesis of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs. This sensor exhibits robust structural integrity, excellent adsorption capacity, and favorable magnetic properties, making it highly suitable for fluorescence-based sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical characterization of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs confirmed their suitability for fluorescence-based sensing applications. Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra (Fig. 3
a) demonstrated that the material exhibited an optimal excitation wavelength at 350 nm, with a corresponding emission peak at 443 nm. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity varied with excitation wavelength within the 310–400 nm range (Fig. 3 b). The intensity increased initially and then decreased, indicating a strong excitation dependence in this range. However, after normalizing the emission spectrum data, the position of the emission peaks remained unchanged (Fig. 3 c), suggesting that the fluorescence emission is independent of the excitation wavelength. This is a desirable property for stable and reproducible detection systems.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra (Fig. 3 d) showed two distinct absorption peaks at 245 nm (B absorption band, π-π* electronic transitions) and 344 nm (R absorption band, n-π* transitions) [35–37]. These transitions are typical for CDs-based materials, indicating the successful incorporation of N, S-CDs into the fluorescence sensor.

The pH stability of the fluorescence sensor was assessed across a wide pH range (1–14) (Fig. 3 e). The fluorescence intensity remained stable between pH = 3 and 12, demonstrating the material's robustness under mildly acidic to moderately alkaline conditions. However, the intensity decreased sharply under extreme pH values (1, 2, 13, and 14), indicating partial degradation or quenching in strong acidic or basic environments. This wide pH tolerance makes the sensor suitable for various environmental and biological applications. To evaluate salt tolerance, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor was measured in NaCl solutions of varying concentrations (0 to 1.0 mol/L) (Fig. 3 f). The fluorescence intensity remained nearly constant across all tested concentrations, indicating excellent salt resistance. This property ensures the sensor's performance in high-salinity environments, such as biological fluids or seawater. Photostability, crucial for long-term fluorescence sensing applications, was also evaluated. The sensor was exposed to continuous ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded every 10 min over 120 min (Fig. 3 g). The results indicated negligible changes in fluorescence intensity over time. This stability ensures consistent performance under prolonged UV exposure, enhancing its reliability for practical use.

In summary, the Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs fluorescence sensor exhibits excellent optical properties, including stable fluorescence emission, robust performance across a wide pH range, good salt tolerance, and exceptional photostability. These characteristics make it a promising candidate for various sensing and environmental monitoring applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid using Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs and mechanism analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The detection capability of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs for p-Hydroxybenzoic acid was systematically analyzed, demonstrating excellent sensitivity and specificity. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of the fluorescence sensor increased with the concentration of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid, reaching its maximum at 600 mg/L, after which it declined (Fig. 4
a). This concentration was identified as optimal for achieving the highest quenching effect. Additionally, the fluorescence quenching efficiency was highest in water (Fig. 4 b), which was selected as the solvent for subsequent fluorescence detection experiments.

To optimize the response time, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly within the first 30 s of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid addition and stabilized after 1.5 min (Fig. 4 c). This rapid detection process led to the establishment of 600 mg/L concentration in water with a response time of 1.5 min as the optimal detection parameters.

Under these optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs decreased progressively with increasing p-Hydroxybenzoic acid concentration, while the fluorescence intensity of the sensor without the molecularly imprinted polymer (NIPs) remained largely unchanged (Fig. 4 d). The linear relationship between the quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) and p-Hydroxybenzoic acid concentration was measured for both MIPs and NIPs (Fig. 4 e). Within the linear range of 0–100 μmol/L, the linear equations for Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs and NIPs were found to be (F0-F)/F0 = 0.00734[p-Hydroxybenzoic acid] + 0.0787 (R2 = 0.9984) and (F0-F)/F0 = 0.00018[p-Hydroxybenzoic acid] − 0.000206 (R2 = 0.9906), respectively. The LOD, calculated as 3σ/k (where σ is the standard deviation of 10 blank samples, and k is the slope of the standard curve), was determined to be 0.37 μmol/L, highlighting the sensor's high sensitivity. The imprinting factor (IF), calculated as the ratio of quenching constants (kMIP/kNIP) was 40.78, indicating a high density of specific recognition sites for p-Hydroxybenzoic acid on the sensor surface. This high IF value confirms the superior recognition capability of the MIPs over non-imprinted counterparts, reinforcing the high specificity and abundance of recognition sites for p-Hydroxybenzoic acid on the magnetic sensor surface.

Further analysis revealed that no new absorption peaks appeared in the UV–Vis spectrum after mixing Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs with p-Hydroxybenzoic acid (Fig. 4 f), ruling out static quenching [38]. The specific binding via hydrogen bonding between p-Hydroxybenzoic acid and the sensor suggests possible electron transfer. As shown in Fig. S5 , p-Hydroxybenzoic acid had –OH group as an electron donor, while carbon dots had electron-withdrawing groups such as sulfonic acid group and carboxyl group, so electrons could move from p-Hydroxybenzoic acid to carbon dots. In addition, compared with NIPs, MIPs showed that the absorption intensity of –OH stretching vibration at 3425 cm−1 and C-H stretching vibration at 2933 cm−1 was significantly enhanced. This further confirmed that the p-Hydroxybenzoic acid molecule induced a change in the luminescent center of the carbon dots through electron transfer, which ultimately led to fluorescence quenching (Fig. 2 b). The absence of overlap between the UV absorption spectra of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the sensor eliminates fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and internal filtration effects (IFE) as potential quenching mechanisms [39]. Additionally, a slight decrease in the fluorescence lifetime were observed from 5.13 ns to 5.03 ns upon the addition of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid, supporting a dynamic quenching mechanism (Fig. 4 g) [40]. In summary, the detection mechanism for p-Hydroxybenzoic acid by Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs involves a synergistic effect of dynamic quenching and electron transfer, contributing to the sensor’s high specificity and sensitivity.

The selectivity of Fe3O4/CDs @MIPs was evaluated by examining their response to various potential interfering substances, including cations (Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+ and so on), anions (Cl-, ClO2 -, F- and so on), amino acids (phenylalanine, cysteine, glycine and so on), and structural analogues of the target analyte, such as m-Hydroxybenzoic acid, o-Hydroxybenzoic acid, phenol, benzoic acid, and p-nitrobenzoic acid. The fluorescence sensor exhibited a significant response only to p-Hydroxybenzoic acid (Fig. 5
(a-b)), with the fluorescence quenching magnitude being far greater than that observed for Fe3O4/CDs@NIPs. Both the MIPs and NIPs showed negligible responses to other interfering substances, demonstrating the high selectivity of the fluorescence sensor.

To assess the anti-interference properties in a complex environment, a mixture of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid (1 mmol/L) and the above interfering substances (10 mmol/L) was introduced to Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs. The fluorescence intensity remained virtually unchanged upon the addition of these interferences (Fig. 5 (c-d)), confirming that the presence of other substances did not affect the sensor’s selectivity and stability during detection. In conclusion, the Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs synthesized in this study demonstrate a high degree of specificity for p-Hydroxybenzoic acid, along with robust anti-interference capabilities, making them highly suitable for accurate detection in complex sample environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs in Real urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: These results demonstrate that the fluorescence sensor exhibits excellent accuracy and sensitivity for detecting p-Hydroxybenzoic acid in complex biological matrices such as urine. In addition, the method has a lower detection limit and a wider linear range than other detection methods (Table S1). The Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs sensor thus holds great potential for practical applications in clinical diagnostics and the monitoring of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid levels.

In this study, we successfully developed a fluorescence sensor based on Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs for the sensitive and selective detection of p-Hydroxybenzoic acid. Characterization techniques, including XRD, FT-IR, and XPS, confirmed the successful synthesis and functionalization of the sensor, highlighting the formation of the silica layer and the incorporation of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The sensor demonstrated optimal performance at a concentration of 600 mg/L in a water-based environment, with a response time of 1.5 min and a detection limit as low as 0.37 μmol/L. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was identified as a synergistic effect of dynamic quenching and electron transfer. Selectivity tests against various cations, anions, amino acids, and structural analogs showed the sensor's high selectivity for p-Hydroxybenzoic acid, demonstrating robust anti-interference capabilities. Spike recovery experiments on urine samples from gastric cancer patients and healthy individuals confirmed the sensor's accuracy, with recovery rates ranging from 95.00 % to 106.67 % and RSDs between 0.76 % and 2.80 %. In conclusion, the Fe3O4/CDs@MIPs sensor offers a reliable and efficient tool for detecting p-Hydroxybenzoic acid in biological samples, presenting significant potential for clinical diagnostics and environmental monitoring applications.

Rui Jie: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Data curation. Xiaochen Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software. Haiyan Qi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tao Jing: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Jun Li: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Yunpeng Zhao: Software. Jinzhi Yu: Software. Chenyu Qi: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172092); the Special Scientific Research Fund of Powerful Province Through Higher Education (GJQSCYH-2022001)；the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (145309525); the Characteristic Discipline of Plant Foods Processing Technology Research Project (Grant No. YSTSXK202307).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113935.

ce:section.ce:para:
